profiles.name: minimal
profiles.description: Fewest changes: only the default-enabled registry policy tweaks — no app or capability removals.

profiles.name: default
profiles.description: Balanced baseline: every catalog entry marked DefaultEnabled (the recommended starting point).

profiles.name: aggressive
profiles.description: Most debloat: the default set plus opt-in grade 1-2 app/capability removals (never community-graded).

profiles.name: gaming
profiles.description: The default set, but Xbox / Game Bar entries (tagged Profiles=@('gaming')) are preserved.

profiles.name: opinionated
profiles.description: The aggressive set plus personal-taste extras (tagged Profiles=@('opinionated')): reversed mouse scroll, Start web-search off, lock-screen Spotlight off, and WSL.

entries.description: Removes the Clipchamp video editor provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Clipchamp is a consumer video editor bundled with Windows 11 that most managed/clean builds do not require; removing the provisioned package prevents auto-install for new users. It can be reinstalled from the Store on demand.
entries.reversal: Reinstall from the Microsoft Store (Clipchamp) or re-add the provisioned package with Add-AppxProvisionedPackage.

entries.description: Removes the Microsoft News (Bing News) provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Bing News is a consumer content/advertising app not needed for a clean baseline; it periodically fetches feed content and notifications. Reinstallable from the Store.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Microsoft News from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the MSN Weather (Bing Weather) provisioned app.
entries.rationale: MSN Weather is a consumer app that is redundant on managed builds and is a common bloatware removal; it is not a Windows component. Reinstallable from the Store.
entries.reversal: Reinstall MSN Weather from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Microsoft Solitaire Collection provisioned app.
entries.rationale: A consumer game bundled with Windows; not required for productivity/managed images and shows ads. Reinstallable from the Store.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Microsoft Solitaire Collection from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Xbox Game Bar overlay provisioned app.
entries.rationale: The Xbox Game Bar overlay is a gaming feature unnecessary on non-gaming/managed builds. Note: fully disabling Game Bar may require the associated registry tweak; this only removes the provisioned app for new users.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Xbox Game Bar from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the legacy Xbox Game Overlay provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Companion overlay component for Xbox gaming features; redundant on managed/non-gaming images.
entries.reversal: Reinstall from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Xbox TCUI (title callable UI) provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Shared Xbox UI component used by gaming titles; not required on managed/non-gaming builds. Some Store games may re-request it.
entries.reversal: Reinstall from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Xbox speech-to-text overlay provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Accessibility overlay tied to Xbox gaming; unnecessary on managed/non-gaming images.
entries.reversal: Reinstall from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the consumer Microsoft Teams (personal / Chat) provisioned app.
entries.rationale: The consumer Teams app (the taskbar Chat) is distinct from Teams for work/school and is commonly removed on managed builds. Removing the provisioned consumer package does not affect the enterprise Teams client, which is installed separately.
entries.reversal: Reinstall the personal Teams app from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Windows Tips (Get Started) provisioned app.
entries.rationale: The Tips app surfaces promotional/onboarding content and is not needed on managed images.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Tips from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Get Help provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Consumer support/contact app that is redundant on managed builds with their own support channels.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Get Help from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Feedback Hub provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Feedback Hub sends diagnostic feedback to Microsoft and is unnecessary on managed/privacy-conscious builds.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Feedback Hub from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Microsoft To Do provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Consumer task app tied to a Microsoft account; not part of a minimal managed baseline. Reinstallable from the Store.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Microsoft To Do from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Power Automate desktop provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Bundled RPA tool that most clean images do not require; can be deployed intentionally where needed.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Power Automate from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Quick Assist remote-help provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Quick Assist enables inbound remote assistance and is a known social-engineering/support-scam vector; removing it from the default image reduces attack surface. Re-add intentionally where remote assistance is a supported workflow.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Quick Assist from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes any King.com Candy Crush titles delivered as provisioned/stub apps.
entries.rationale: Third-party promoted games installed via the Windows consumer content-delivery experience; pure advertising bloat with no productivity value. Removed if present; skipped as NotApplicable if absent.
entries.reversal: Reinstall from the Microsoft Store if desired.

entries.description: Removes the Xbox app (Microsoft.GamingApp) provisioned package.
entries.rationale: The Xbox app (game library, Game Pass, cloud gaming) is a consumer gaming client unnecessary on managed/non-gaming builds. Tagged Profiles=@('gaming') so the gaming profile keeps it; removed by the default profile for a clean baseline. Reinstallable from the Store.
entries.reversal: Reinstall the Xbox app from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Phone Link (Microsoft.YourPhone) provisioned app.
entries.rationale: Phone Link mirrors a paired phone (calls, messages, photos) and is a consumer feature not needed on a minimal managed baseline. Reinstallable from the Store.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Phone Link from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the Microsoft Family (Family Safety) provisioned app.
entries.rationale: The Family app manages parental controls / family group settings tied to a consumer Microsoft account; it is not part of a managed baseline. Reinstallable from the Store.
entries.reversal: Reinstall the Family app from the Microsoft Store.

entries.description: Removes the LinkedIn provisioned/promoted app if present.
entries.rationale: LinkedIn is a third-party app promoted via the Windows consumer content-delivery experience and pinned to Start on consumer images; advertising bloat on a clean baseline. Removed if present; skipped as NotApplicable if absent.
entries.reversal: Reinstall LinkedIn from the Microsoft Store if desired.

entries.description: Removes the WhatsApp provisioned/promoted app if present.
entries.rationale: WhatsApp is a third-party app promoted via the Windows consumer content-delivery experience and pinned to Start on consumer images; advertising bloat on a clean baseline. Removed if present; skipped as NotApplicable if absent.
entries.reversal: Reinstall WhatsApp from the Microsoft Store if desired.

entries.description: Removes the provisioned Microsoft Edge browser packaging (opt-in, impactful).
entries.rationale: Edge is a deeply integrated Windows component and Microsoft services it as part of the OS; removal can break WebView2-dependent apps and Windows features. Provided strictly as an opt-in choice for users who deploy an alternative browser. The offline-removal technique is community-documented rather than an officially supported single procedure (EvidenceGrade 3), so it MUST stay opt-in.
entries.reversal: Reinstall Microsoft Edge from https://www.microsoft.com/edge or via the Edge stable installer.

entries.description: Removes the WordPad optional feature.
entries.rationale: WordPad is deprecated by Microsoft and no longer receives updates; it is not installed by default on new Windows 11 images. Removing it (when present) trims a legacy, unmaintained component. Users needing rich text can use Word or a third-party editor.
entries.reversal: Add-WindowsCapability -Online -Name Microsoft.Windows.WordPad~~~~0.0.1.0 (source required).

entries.description: Removes the Steps Recorder (PSR) optional feature.
entries.rationale: Steps Recorder is deprecated by Microsoft and slated for removal; it captures screen steps and is unnecessary on managed images. Deprecated-feature status makes removal low-risk.
entries.reversal: Add-WindowsCapability -Online -Name App.StepsRecorder~~~~0.0.1.0 (source required).

entries.description: Removes the Windows Fax and Scan optional feature.
entries.rationale: Legacy fax/scan tooling that most modern managed builds do not use; removing it trims rarely used components. Opt-in for environments that still fax.
entries.reversal: Add-WindowsCapability -Online -Name Print.Fax.Scan~~~~0.0.1.0 (source required).

entries.description: Removes the legacy Windows Media Player (WMP) optional feature.
entries.rationale: The legacy WMP is superseded by the modern Media Player app; some environments still rely on the legacy player, so this is opt-in to avoid breaking media workflows.
entries.reversal: Add-WindowsCapability -Online -Name Media.WindowsMediaPlayer~~~~0.0.12.0 (source required).

entries.description: Enables the Windows Subsystem for Linux optional feature on the offline image.
entries.rationale: Pre-enables the WSL platform feature so the finished image ships WSL-ready. This offline step only turns on the OS feature; the actual WSL2 kernel and the chosen Linux distribution are installed online on first boot (`wsl --install`/`wsl --update`), which is a Microsoft platform constraint, not a limitation of this tool. Opt-in because it adds attack surface not everyone wants.
entries.reversal: Disable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName Microsoft-Windows-Subsystem-Linux (or omit the feature-wsl catalog id).

entries.description: Enables the Virtual Machine Platform optional feature (WSL2 dependency) on the offline image.
entries.rationale: WSL2 runs a lightweight utility VM and requires the Virtual Machine Platform feature. Enable it alongside feature-wsl for a WSL2-capable image. Like feature-wsl the actual WSL2 kernel is fetched online on first boot. Opt-in.
entries.reversal: Disable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName VirtualMachinePlatform (or omit the feature-vmplatform catalog id).

entries.description: Disables Windows Recall by setting the DisableAIDataAnalysis policy (turns off saving Recall snapshots).
entries.rationale: Recall periodically captures screenshots of user activity, which is a significant privacy/data-at-rest concern for managed builds. Microsoft documents DisableAIDataAnalysis as the supported policy to turn off saving snapshots. Spec FR-007 mandates this be disabled by default; it is fully reversible.
entries.reversal: Delete the DisableAIDataAnalysis value (or set it to 0) under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WindowsAI.

entries.description: Disables the Widgets board (weather/stock/news feed) via the AllowNewsAndInterests policy.
entries.rationale: The Widgets feed (weather, stocks, news) fetches internet content and adds taskbar surface area not wanted on managed builds. Microsoft documents AllowNewsAndInterests under Policies\Microsoft\Dsh as the control. Spec FR-007 mandates disabling weather/stock Widgets by default; reversible.
entries.reversal: Set AllowNewsAndInterests to 1 (or delete it) under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Dsh.

entries.description: Disables Windows consumer features (auto-installed suggested/promoted apps such as third-party games).
entries.rationale: The consumer content-delivery experience silently installs promoted third-party apps (e.g. Candy Crush) and suggestions. Disabling it prevents re-appearance of bloat after debloating and is the supported enterprise control.
entries.reversal: Set DisableWindowsConsumerFeatures to 0 (or delete it) under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent.

entries.description: Turns off the per-user advertising ID used to personalize ads across apps.
entries.rationale: The advertising ID enables cross-app ad tracking. Microsoft documents DisabledByGroupPolicy under AdvertisingInfo as the control (Policy CSP Privacy/DisableAdvertisingId). A privacy-improving, reversible default.
entries.reversal: Set DisabledByGroupPolicy to 0 (or delete it) under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AdvertisingInfo.

entries.description: Sets the diagnostic-data (telemetry) policy to the lowest level (Security/Required).
entries.rationale: Reduces diagnostic data sent to Microsoft. Note: on Pro the effective floor is "Required/Basic" (the 0/Security level is fully honored only on Enterprise/Education), which Microsoft documents; the policy is still the correct, supported control and is reversible.
entries.reversal: Set AllowTelemetry to the desired level (1=Required, 3=Optional) or delete it under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DataCollection.

entries.description: Disables Cortana via the AllowCortana policy.
entries.rationale: Cortana is largely deprecated and unnecessary on managed builds; disabling it reduces background voice/assistant surface. Microsoft documents AllowCortana in Policy CSP Search. Reversible.
entries.reversal: Set AllowCortana to 1 (or delete it) under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Search.

entries.description: Prevents Windows from using diagnostic data to show tailored experiences (personalized tips/ads/suggestions).
entries.rationale: Stops Microsoft from using diagnostic data to personalize tips, ads, and recommendations in the shell. Microsoft documents the corresponding Experience/AllowTailoredExperiencesWithDiagnosticData policy. Privacy-improving and reversible.
entries.reversal: Set DisableTailoredExperiencesWithDiagnosticData to 0 (or delete it) under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent.

entries.description: Disables web/Bing suggestions in the Start menu and taskbar search box.
entries.rationale: Removes internet-backed search suggestions from Start/search for a more local, private search experience. This exact value is community-documented rather than in a single authoritative Microsoft page (EvidenceGrade 3), so it is opt-in only.
entries.reversal: Set DisableSearchBoxSuggestions to 0 (or delete it) under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Search.

entries.description: Disables Windows Spotlight lock-screen "fun facts/ads" overlays for new user profiles.
entries.rationale: Spotlight lock-screen overlays surface suggestions/ads. Applied to the DEFAULT hive so new profiles inherit it. The specific per-user ContentDeliveryManager value is community-documented (EvidenceGrade 3), so it is opt-in only.
entries.reversal: Set RotatingLockScreenOverlayEnabled to 1 (or delete it) under DEFAULT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\ContentDeliveryManager.

entries.description: Prevents the OneDrive sync client from being used, via the supported DisableFileSyncNGSC policy (opt-in).
entries.rationale: Microsoft documents the "Prevent the usage of OneDrive for file storage" policy (DisableFileSyncNGSC = 1) as the supported way to disable OneDrive. This is the reversible, low-risk part of "removing OneDrive" from an image; it does not delete the OneDrive binary. Provided opt-in per FR-008.
entries.reversal: Set DisableFileSyncNGSC to 0 (or delete it) under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\OneDrive.

entries.description: Reverses the mouse wheel scroll direction (macOS-style "natural" scrolling) by setting FlipFlopWheel=1 on every HID mouse via a first-boot RunOnce command.
entries.rationale: FlipFlopWheel is the Microsoft-documented per-device control that inverts wheel scroll direction (see Microsoft's "How to reverse the mouse wheel scrolling direction" whitepaper, wheel.docx). It lives under each mouse's SYSTEM\...\Enum\HID\<device>\Device Parameters key, which is only populated by PnP at first boot and therefore cannot be written into a generalized offline image. Baking a machine RunOnce (also a Microsoft-documented mechanism: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/setupapi/run-and-runonce-registry-keys) that sets FlipFlopWheel=1 on all enumerated HID mice at first logon is the reliable, reversible way to apply it. Kept opt-in (DefaultEnabled=$false) because reversed scrolling is a personal-taste preference, not a general improvement.
entries.reversal: Remove the !WimReverseMouseScroll value under SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce before first boot, or after boot set FlipFlopWheel to 0 (or delete it) under each mouse's SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\HID\<device>\Device Parameters key.
